pubmed_links: [Watanabe et al. (2016) Rare truncating variations and risk of schizophrenia](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26706132/); [Scelsi et al. (2018) Genetic study of multimodal imaging Alzheimer's disease progression score implicates novel loci](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29860282/); [Wainberg et al. (2023) Shared genetic risk loci between Alzheimer's disease and related dementias, Parkinson's disease, and ALS](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37328865/)
summary: LCORL has been implicated as a candidate gene in schizophrenia via a rare truncating variant (R7fsX53) identified in whole-exome sequencing of multiplex families, and an LCORL eQTL on chromosome 4 has been associated with Alzheimer's disease progression and identified as a shared risk locus across neurodegenerative conditions. Evidence linking LCORL to neurodevelopmental disorders such as autism or intellectual disability is currently absent from the literature. (novel candidate)
